nodes.text: Cosmic, Without Rick
The keys are heavy in a way Maya had not, at sixteen, expected keys to be heavy.

There are six of them on the ring, plus the green plastic tab that says COSMIC in Rick's handwriting from sometime in the late 1990s. The keys are — front door, back door, register drawer, safe in the back office, long-box storage room, mailbox at the end of the strip. The mailbox key is the smallest. The safe key is the longest. The front and back doors are the ones Rick had, on Sunday at noon when he handed her the ring, walked her through twice — front door deadbolt first, then the bottom lock, then the chain inside if she is the last one out, which today she is going to be.
She has been at the door for forty seconds.

She is doing the thing she has been doing every time she has been entrusted with something this summer — hesitating at the threshold of the entrustment for slightly longer than is necessary, because she is, every time, having to actually decide that the entrustment is real and not a misunderstanding she is being permitted to continue.
She turns the key.
The deadbolt slides.
She turns the second key.
The bottom lock turns.
She pushes the door open.

The shop at nine forty-eight on a Tuesday morning in mid-August is doing the quiet a comic shop does in the hour before opening. The brass bell above the door — the one from the 1994 estate sale that the previous owner had, Rick says, heard something in — chimes in its small dry way. The fluorescent tubes overhead are not yet on. The light in the shop is the slanting ten-AM light coming through the painted Galactus on the front window and laying the painted Galactus across the floor in long purple bars.
She locks the door behind her. She flips the CLOSED sign to face inward. She does not, yet, turn it.
She crosses to the back office.
Rick had left, on the desk, the spiral notebook he had been keeping for her since Sunday. The notebook is open. It has, in his handwriting, a list of things she might need to know.
She has, since Sunday afternoon, read the list four times.
She reads it again.
Kid:
— Diamond truck Tuesday usually 2pm. Could be late. Could be Wednesday. They'll call. Sign for it. Don't open the boxes till you and Curtis have a minute together. He knows the routine.
— Subscriptions get pulled Wednesday morning. The list is in the binder under the register. You'll see. Curtis can show you if you forget. He won't like being asked. Ask anyway.
— Mrs. Delacroix comes in Wednesday for her Archie. New issue is in the back, came in last week. Top of the new-arrivals box, marked.
— Hal will call Thursday morning. He always does on the weeks I'm out. He just wants to check. Tell him things are fine. He'll believe you.
— If Curtis asks about the Friday deposit slip, tell him I have it. I do. He'll grumble. Let him grumble.
— Wren is going to ask for Saturday off. Say yes. The girlfriend's birthday. I'd already told her she could but I forgot to tell you.
— Speak & Spell does what it does. Let it. Don't mention it to anybody who comes in. If it does something while a customer is in the shop, walk to the back and unplug it. You know which outlet.
— You've got this, kid. Call me only if it's on fire. Even then, decide if it can wait.
She closes the notebook.
She puts it in the top drawer of the desk.

She turns on the fluorescent tubes. She turns on the back-office computer. She takes the deposit slip from the cash register from yesterday — Sunday's, which Curtis had run because Curtis always does Sunday — and confirms the handwritten total at the bottom matches her own count from yesterday afternoon. The total matches. She files the slip in the green folder in the second drawer.
She crosses to the front. She unlocks the door. She flips the sign to OPEN.
She does not, immediately, return to the back. She stands at the counter for a moment.

The shop, opened, makes a different sound than the shop, closed. The HVAC, set for daytime, comes on with its standard clunk. The Galactus light on the floor shifts as the morning sun moves. The brass bell hangs still above the door.
She goes to the back office.
She makes a coffee from the Mr. Coffee on the file cabinet that has, by Rick's report, been the Mr. Coffee since 2008.
She sits at the desk.
She waits for the day.
Curtis comes in at ten fifteen.

Curtis is fifty-six. He is a tall thin man with a short gray ponytail and a t-shirt that says MIRACLEMAN in the old yellow-on-black design from the 1985 Eclipse run. He has, in the four months Maya has been at the shop, worn perhaps eleven different t-shirts in regular rotation, all from runs Maya has had to look up afterward. The MIRACLEMAN one is the one Curtis wears on Tuesdays. He has, in his hand, a thermos and a copy of yesterday's Sentinel.
Morning, Maya.
Morning, Curtis.

He goes behind the counter without breaking stride. He sets the thermos under the register. He puts the Sentinel on the small shelf where Rick keeps his when he is in. He checks the register screen — the morning glance every working manager makes at a shop's till — and registers, by what he sees there, that the till is set to where it should be.
Coffee?
I have one in the back.
Okay.

Curtis goes about his morning. He puts up the new shelf-talkers for the Tuesday rotation. He moves three books from the back-issue boxes that he had marked yesterday. He does not speak to Maya for the next forty minutes.
She does not, in the forty minutes, leave the back office. She has been told by Rick to give Curtis the morning to himself. She gives him the morning.
By eleven the shop has had six customers. By eleven forty-five, nine. By noon, twelve.

The Tuesday flow is not the flow of a Wednesday or a Thursday or a Saturday — but it is a flow. Maya, at the back-office door, watches Curtis handle the first hour and a half from her doorway. Curtis is, in his work, what Rick had said he was on Sunday: not chatty, not warm, technically perfect. Curtis rings. Curtis bags. Curtis greets the customer. Curtis does not, in the greeting, make small talk that is not required.
Maya, at the doorway, learns by watching.
She eats her sandwich at the desk at twelve fourteen.
The phone rings.

It is Diamond Distribution. The dispatcher is a woman named Delia, who Maya has not spoken to before but who introduces herself with the efficient warmth of a person who has been doing distribution dispatch for thirty years.
Honey, just a heads-up. Driver's running behind. Could be Tuesday very late, could be Wednesday morning. Sign-out window's nine to five, you know the drill?
Yeah. Thanks for letting me know.
You're new. Are you the new —
I'm Maya. I'm Rick's — I'm covering for Rick this week.
Rick at the con?
Yeah.
You tell him Delia says hi. Tell him I said the boxes are heavier this month. He's gonna want to send the strong one out to the truck.
I'll tell him.
Delia hangs up.
Maya finishes her sandwich.

She does not, immediately, tell Curtis the truck is going to be late. She does not need to. Curtis will, by his own clock, have already factored in that the truck is sometimes Wednesday, and Curtis does not like being told things he has already factored.
She sits at the desk.

She has, in the past two hours, fielded a delivery call from a thirty-year veteran of comics distribution who treated her like an adult. She has been part of the invisible network of comic-shop logistics that runs underneath every shop in the country. She is inside it now. Not because of Frasier. Not because of any of that. Because Rick is in Dallas, and Delia called the number she was given, and Maya picked up the phone.
She is, she registers, slightly proud of herself.
She does not have anybody to be proud to. The pride is an interior thing she is holding by herself at twelve twenty-two.
The interior thing is fine. The not-having-anybody is — for today, for the next two hours — fine.
She gets up. She goes back to the front to give Curtis his lunch break.
Wren arrives at one PM.

Wren is twenty-eight. Wren has, this morning, dyed the front section of her hair a pale lavender Maya had not seen yesterday afternoon when she had left her shift. Wren is wearing the canvas overshirt she always wears, which has, on the back, a faded patch from a band Maya has heard of but has never listened to — Bratmobile, Olympia, 1991 — that Wren had sewn on herself and that has, in the years since, become the thing Wren is most reliably identifiable by from a distance.
Morning, Maya. Hair okay?
Hair's great.
Yeah?
Yeah.
Cool. I did it last night. I needed — I needed to do something with my hands.
Yeah.
Wren goes behind the counter. She drops her bag. She looks at the register screen the same way Curtis had at ten fifteen.
Maya. Quick thing.
Yeah.
Saturday. I — Rick said it would be okay. I'm going to San Marcos. It's Liz's birthday. I want to go down Friday night, come back Sunday. Are you — is that okay?
Maya, who had been told by Rick to say yes, says: "Yes."
Yes.
Wren exhales.
Okay. I — thank you. I can — I can pick up an extra shift this week if you need. Wednesday or Thursday.
I'm okay this week. Rick said you'd ask. He told me to say yes. He said he forgot to tell you.
Wren laughs, short.
That sounds like Rick.
Yeah.
Wren looks at her.
You good, kid? First day solo?
I'm good.
Curtis behaving?
Curtis is being Curtis.
That's Curtis behaving. He's a good guy. Just — he gets weird when Rick is out. He'll loosen up by Thursday.
Okay.
Wren takes her bag to the small employee cubby behind the counter. She comes back out.
Hey. Lunch later. I want to talk to you about a thing.
Yeah?
Not a — not a thing-thing. Just a thing. Tell you at three when Curtis takes his break.
Yeah.
Wren nods.
Curtis takes his lunch at one fifteen. He goes to the small Salvadoran place across the strip mall. He is back at one forty-eight.

Maya and Wren run the floor together for the half-hour Curtis is gone. The floor is steady — five customers in the half-hour, all regulars, none with anything weird. Wren rings. Maya bags. The two of them work the way they had been working since June, when Wren had been first to learn that Maya was actually competent at the register and had stopped, after week two, doing the rings herself when they overlapped.
At one forty-eight Curtis comes back. He goes behind the counter. He takes over. Wren takes Maya to the back office.
The back office is small. The desk is small. The file cabinet is small. The Speak & Spell is on the shelf above the file cabinet, dim.
Wren closes the door.
Wren sits on the edge of the desk.
Maya.
Yeah.

I am about to tell you something I have not told anybody else who works here. I am telling you because I think you need to know it for the week. I am also telling you because you have been, since June, the kind of kid who I have decided I would tell things to. I want you to know that you can not, after I tell you, do anything with it. The not-doing is the price of the telling.
Maya looks at her.
Okay.
Wren takes a breath.
Wren tells Maya the thing.
Wren. I'm sorry.
Yeah. Me too.
Are you —

I'm okay. Liz is okay. The thing is — the thing is in motion. The thing will be in motion for a while. By the time Rick is back I will have a better answer than I do today. I am telling you today because today is the day I needed to tell somebody and you are the person who is here.
Okay.
Curtis doesn't know.
I won't tell him.
He might guess. Curtis guesses. If he asks you about my face, tell him I had a thing on the phone this morning. Don't tell him what the thing was.
Yeah.
Thank you, Maya.
Wren. I'm glad you told me. I'm sorry.
Me too.
She gets off the desk.
She opens the back-office door.
She goes back to the floor.

Maya stays in the back office for a minute. She sits at the desk. She does not, in the minute, do anything with what Wren has told her. She files it. She is going to file it again, in different drawers, over the course of the rest of the day and the rest of the week and the rest of the next month. The first filing is the one in the back office at one fifty-three PM with the door open and Curtis at the register thirty feet away.
She gets up.
She goes back to the floor.
The afternoon is busier than the morning.

By three the shop has had nine customers. By four-thirty, fourteen. By six, twenty-one. Maya, at the register, finds — between customers — that her hands have begun to know the motions of the register and the bag and the receipt without her conscious attention, and that the lack of conscious attention is freeing the part of her interior that has been, all morning, vigilant about being watched by Curtis.
She rings.
She bags.
She receipts.
She greets the customer who comes in. She wishes them well when they leave.

By six the shop has the late-afternoon hum of a small shop on a Tuesday — three customers browsing, two at the back issues, one at the manga wall. Curtis is at the counter. Wren is in the back unboxing a mid-week order from a small-press distributor that is not Diamond. Maya is at the indie rack putting up the new-arrivals shelf-talkers.
The Speak & Spell, on the shelf in the back office, does not glow.
It has not glowed all day.

Maya has, every time she has been in the back office, looked at it. The red speaker grille is dim. The crack in the lower-left corner of the housing is the same crack it has been since 2014. The Speak & Spell today is just a Speak & Spell.
She does not know whether this means anything.
She files the not-glowing along with everything else she has filed today.
Curtis takes his second break at six twenty-two.
He goes to the back office. He sits at the desk. He drinks from the thermos. He reads the Sentinel.
Maya, at the counter, has the front of the shop.
At six forty-one Curtis comes back out.
Maya.
She turns.
The Diamond invoice from last Friday. The one that came in just before Rick left. The one that's sitting on the desk in the green folder. Did Rick tell you what to do with it.
He said you would handle it.
Curtis is silent for a beat.
Did he write that down.
She thinks. She had read the notebook list four times. The Diamond invoice from Friday is not, she is fairly sure, in the list.
He didn't write it down. He said it Sunday at noon. He said Curtis will run the Friday invoice through, he knows the schedule.
Curtis nods, slowly.
Okay. I'll run it through tonight before I leave.
He returns to the indie rack.

The exchange has been, on its surface, transactional. The exchange has also been — Maya registers, with the careful attention Wren had three hours earlier equipped her for — a man checking whether the kid running the shop has been entrusted with the paper part of the operation or only the floor part. Curtis has, in asking, confirmed for himself that Rick has not given Maya the invoice.
She does not, in the moment, do anything with the relief.
She rings up the next customer.
At seven fifty-eight Wren comes out from the back.
Curtis. I'm out. Big day Wednesday. Maya, you closing?
Yeah.
You good?
I'm good.
Curtis. Behave.
I always behave.
Wren laughs. She picks up her bag. She crosses to the door. She turns at the door.
Maya. Text me if anything weird happens.
I will.
Wren leaves. The bell chimes.
The shop, at eight oh-one, has three customers left — two in the back issues, one at the new-arrivals shelf.
Last call in five.
The customer at the new-arrivals shelf nods. The two in the back issues say yeah, gimme a sec.
By eight oh-eight the shop is empty.
Curtis goes to the back to run the Friday invoice through.

Maya, at the counter, starts the close. She counts the till. She separates the cash from the card receipts. She runs the register report. She pulls the bag-and-board waste from the bin under the register. She wipes the counter. She turns off the back fluorescents in sections, leaving the front bank for last. She does the walk-through of the aisles — her own walk-through, which she has been doing without being asked for two months — to make sure the shelves are face-front and the displays are square. The walk-through is not in Rick's notebook. It is a thing she has been doing because she has noticed, doing it, that the morning is easier when the close-night has been thorough.
She comes back to the counter at eight twenty-seven.
Curtis is at the desk in the back office. The light is on. He is, by the sound of the keys, doing the invoice in the QuickBooks file Rick has been running since 2003.
She waits.
Curtis finishes at eight forty-one.
He comes out of the back office. He has his thermos and his Sentinel. He has the green folder with the Friday invoice now timestamped and filed.
He puts the folder on the counter.
Done.
Thanks, Curtis.
He pauses briefly.
Maya.
Yeah.
He looks at her for a beat that is, she registers, not the beat of his ordinary closing-time pause. The beat is one syllable longer than usual.
You're doing fine, kid.
She blinks.
She had not, in the day, expected this.
Curtis. Thank you.
Don't make it a thing.
He picks up his stuff.
He goes to the door.
He turns.
Wren — Wren told you stuff at lunch. I saw her face. I — I want you to know. Whatever she told you, it's — it's not your problem. I want you to know that.
Maya looks at him.
Curtis. I know.
Curtis nods.
Good kid.
Curtis leaves.
The bell chimes.
Maya, alone in the shop, stands at the counter for a long minute.

Curtis has just told her, in three sentences, more than he has ever told her. He told her by deciding to mention it. He told her he had registered Wren's lunch conversation with her, that he had a guess as to what it had been about, and that he wanted her to know that whatever it had been about was not her problem. He told her, in his way, that he is on her side. He gave her the assurance an adult gives a kid when the adult has decided the kid does not need to be carrying the adult's problem for the adult.
Curtis is going to be okay. The week is going to be okay.

She is going to keep doing her job. She is going to let Curtis keep doing his. At the end of the week Rick is going to come back and the shape of the thing is going to be the same shape it was before, except with one more person — her — knowing what the shape is.
That is, today, what she can do.

She locks the front door. She turns the OPEN sign to CLOSED. She does the walk to the back. She turns off the front fluorescents. She turns off the back-office computer. She picks up her bag from under the desk. She turns off the back-office light.
She goes out the back door. She locks it. She tests the deadbolt. She tests it again.
She walks to her bike at the rack.
